Diabetes Ther
https://doi.org/10.1007/s13300-026-01884-2
SYSTEMATIC REVIEW
Treatment Options forPatients withMaturity‑Onset
Diabetes oftheYoung (MODY): ASystematic Review
ofLiterature: 2026 Update
LucaZagaroli· AlessioDiBerardino· FrancescoPetragnano· SerenaLustri·
IreneFasciani· MarioRossi· MarcoG.Baroni· MaurizioDelvecchio
Received: March 7, 2026 / Accepted: May 19, 2026
© The Author(s) 2026
ABSTRACT
Introduction: Maturity-onset diabetes of the
young (MODY) is a rare monogenic form of dia-
betes characterized by impaired insulin secretion
and genetic and clinical heterogeneity. Accurate
molecular diagnosis enables precision medicine
by guiding gene-specic treatment, improving
glycemic control, and avoiding unnecessary
therapies. Since our previous systematic review
in 2020, new studies have further clarified
therapeutic strategies and emerging treatment
options. This review updates the evidence on
pharmacological management of MODY and its
clinical implications.
Methods: A systematic review was conducted
following PRISMA 2020 recommendations. Pub-
Med was searched for studies published between
April 2020 and October 2025. Eligible studies
included individuals with genetically conrmed
MODY receiving pharmacological treatment,
therapeutic switches, or adjunctive therapies.
Data were extracted and synthesized qualita-
tively due to heterogeneity in study design and
outcome reporting.
Results: Ninety-one studies were included.
Strong evidence conrms sulfonylureas (SUs)
as rst-line therapy in Hepatocyte Nuclear Fac-
tor 1A (HNF1A)-, Hepatocyte Nuclear Factor 4A
(HNF4A)-, ATP-binding cassette transporter sub-
family C member 8 (ABCC8)-, and potassium
inwardly rectifying channel subfamily J mem-
ber 11 (KCNJ11)-MODY, frequently enabling
improved glycemic control and transition from
insulin. Glucokinase (GCK)-MODY remains
managed without pharmacological treatment
outside pregnancy, but dorzagliatin, a novel
agent acting as a glucokinase activator, may fur-
ther expand precision treatment approaches.
Insulin remains the mainstay of treatment in
Hepatocyte Nuclear Factor 1B (HNF1B)- and
Insulin (INS)-MODY, although adjunctive ther-
apies may provide benets. While SUs are sup-
ported by consistent observational evidence, the
Prior Publication: This manuscript is not based on work
that has been previously presented/published.
Supplementary Information The online version
contains supplementary material available at
https:// doi. org/ 10. 1007/ s13300- 026- 01884-2.
L.Zagaroli
Unit ofPediatrics, San Salvatore Hospital, ASL 1
Abruzzo, L’Aquila, Italy
A.DiBerardino· F.Petragnano· S.Lustri· I.Fasciani·
M.Rossi· M.Delvecchio(*)
Department ofBiotechnological andApplied
Clinical Sciences (DISCAB), University ofL’Aquila,
L’Aquila, Italy
e-mail: mdelvecchio75@gmail.com
M.G.Baroni
Department ofClinical Medicine, Public Health, Life
andEnvironmental Sciences (MeSVA), University
ofL’Aquila, L’Aquila, Italy
Diabetes Ther
role of incretin-based therapies and sodium-glu-
cose co-transporter 2 inhibitors remains explor-
atory, largely based on small studies and case
reports.
Conclusions: A major limitation of the avail-
able evidence is the predominance of small, het-
erogeneous observational studies, which limits
the strength and generalizability of therapeu-
tic recommendations. Recent literature largely
conrms existing genotype-guided treatment
recommendations while suggesting expanded
therapeutic options for selected MODY subtypes.
Precision medicine based on molecular diagnosis
remains essential for optimal management.
Keywords: Maturity-onset diabetes of the
young (MODY); Monogenic diabetes; Precision
medicine; Sulfonylureas; Genotype-guided
therapy; Hepatocyte nuclear factor 1A (HNF1A)-
MODY; Hepatocyte nuclear factor 4A (HNF4A)-
MODY; Glucokinase (GCK)-MODY; Incretin-
based therapies; Sodium-glucose co-transporter
2 (SGLT2) inhibitors
Key Summary Points
Maturity-onset diabetes of the young
(MODY) is a paradigmatic example of preci-
sion medicine, in which molecular diagno-
sis enables genotype-guided therapy and
optimization of clinical management.
Sulfonylureas remain the rst-line phar-
macological treatment for several MODY
subtypes, particularly.
Hepatocyte Nuclear Factor 1A (HNF1A)-,
Hepatocyte Nuclear Factor 4A (HNF4A)-,
ATP-binding cassette transporter sub-family
C member 8 (ABCC8)-, and potassium
inwardly rectifying channel subfamily J
member 11 (KCNJ11)-MODY, often allow-
ing transition from insulin with improved
glycemic control.
Glucokinase (GCK)-MODY usually requires
no pharmacological treatment outside
pregnancy, emphasizing the importance
of genetic diagnosis to avoid unnecessary
lifelong therapy.
Recent evidence highlights the potential
role of incretin-based therapies and sodium-
glucose co-transporter inhibitors as adjunc-
tive treatments in selected patients with
progressive β-cell dysfunction or insulin
resistance.
Most available evidence derives from obser-
vational studies, underscoring the need for
international collaborative research and
prospective trials to strengthen genotype-
guided treatment strategies.
INTRODUCTION
Maturity-onset diabetes of the young (MODY)
is a rare monogenic form of diabetes that
accounts for approximately 1–6% of children
and adolescents with diabetes [1, 2]. A geno-
type-rst approach has conrmed that MODY
variants were present in 1 in 1052 individu-
als and accounted for 1.48% of diabetes cases
diagnosed before age 40 [3]. It encompasses
clinically and genetically heterogeneous disor-
ders caused by single-gene defects that impair
β-cell function and lead to mild to severe insu-
lin deciency. MODY is a paradigmatic setting
for precision medicine: a conrmed molecular
diagnosis can guide glucose-monitoring, avoid
unnecessary therapies (e.g., insulin when not
indicated), and inform gene-specic treatment,
genetic counseling, and prognosis [4]. Although
an onset before 25years of age has traditionally
been considered, increasing evidence shows that
diagnosis may occur later.
Historically, up to 14 MODY subtypes were
described, although most cases arise from vari-
ants in Hepatocyte Nuclear Factor 1A (HNF1A),
Glucokinase (GCK), and Hepatocyte Nuclear
Factor 4A (HNF4A) genes, with prevalence dif-
fering across cohorts [5–8].
Diabetes Ther
Phenotype can vary to some extent among
carriers of the same variant and even within
the same pedigree. Subtypes differ by age at
onset, genotype, glycemic patterns, and extra-
pancreatic features -such as renal cysts in
Hepatocyte Nuclear Factor 1B (HNF1B)-MODY
or neonatal hypoglycemia and macrosomia in
HNF4A-MODY. Accurate molecular diagnosis
is linked to better outcomes by enabling gene-
tailored therapy, mitigation of complication
risk, targeted family counseling, and reduction
of stigma associated with inappropriate treat-
ment. The importance of accurate diagnosis
and treatment adjustment has been outlined
by numerous authors and international guide-
lines [2, 9], even if trial evidence is limited.
The risk of micro- and macrovascular compli-
cations relates to the degree and duration of
hyperglycemia and not to the etiology of the
disease. As a result, achieving glycemic targets
reduces the onset and progression of vascular
complications [10, 11].
Advances in genetic testing, particularly next-
generation sequencing, have improved diagnos-
tic accuracy and reduced misclassication as
type 1 (T1D) or type 2 (T2D) diabetes, improv-
ing recognition and management of monogenic
diabetes. The patient’s perspective reported by
Thanabalasingham etal. [12] is meaningful to
understand the difculties in some settings to
properly diagnose monogenic diabetes and pro-
vide the appropriate treatment, and the patients’
thoughts. It highlights how an etiological diag-
nosis improves quality of life and blood glucose
control.
The increasing number of diagnoses has raised
clinical awareness of this condition, emphasiz-
ing the need for targeted therapeutic approaches
within the framework of precision medicine. In
this context, research on monogenic diabetes
has expanded in recent years, with several rel-
evant studies investigating potential pharmaco-
logical treatment strategies for these patients. In
2020, we published a review summarizing gene-
specic treatment evidence for clinicians [13].
Therefore, 5years later, we undertook a com-
prehensive revision of the review to incorporate
emerging evidence from the scientic literature,
focusing on practical implications for the preci-
sion treatment of monogenic diabetes.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Study Design
We conducted a systematic review in accord-
ance with PRISMA 2020 recommendations
(qualitative synthesis; no meta-analysis
planned a priori). We searched PubMed for
studies published from April 16, 2020 (date of
the last study included in the previous review
[13]) to October 11, 2025 (search last run
on October 11, 2025). The full search string
was: ((mody[Title/Abstract]) OR (maturity-
onset diabetes of the young[Title/Abstract])
OR (HNF4A[Title/Abstract]) OR (GCK[Title/
Abstract]) OR (HNF1A[Title/Abstract]) OR
(Pancreatic and Duodenal Homeobox 1
-PDX1- [Title/Abstract]) OR (HNF1B[Title/
Abstract]) OR (Neurogenic Differentiation 1
-NEUROD1-[Title/Abstract]) OR (Krüppel-like
factor 11 -KLF11-[Title/Abstract]) OR (Car-
boxyl Ester Lipase -CEL-[Title/Abstract]) OR
(Paired Box4 -PAX4-[Title/Abstract]) OR (Insu-
lin –INS-[Title/Abstract]) OR (B-Lymphocyte
Kinase -BLK-[Title/Abstract]) OR (ATP-bind-
ing cassette transporter sub-family C mem-
ber 8 -ABCC8-[Title/Abstract]) OR (potassium
inwardly rectifying channel subfamily J mem-
ber 11 -KCNJ11-[Title/Abstract]) OR (adapter
protein containing PH domain -APPL1-[Title/
Abstract])) AND ((treatment[Title/Abstract]) OR
(therapy[Title/Abstract])). PubMed was selected
as the primary database for its extensive cover-
age of the biomedical literature. To minimize
missing studies, we also manually screened the
reference lists of included articles, identifying
additional relevant publications. This approach
was consistent with the search strategy adopted
in our previously published systematic review
[13], in order to ensure methodological conti-
nuity and comparability between the original
review and the present update. The selection of
articles was conducted according to the PICOS
framework as follows:
•
Population: individuals with genetically
conrmed MODY (any subtype), children
or adults.
Diabetes Ther
•
Intervention/Exposure: any antihypergly-
cemic treatment or therapy modification
after genetic diagnosis with reported out-
comes.
•
Comparators: any (including within-
patient before/after) or none for single-
arm/case designs.
•
Outcomes: glycemic outcomes (e.g., hemo-
globin A1c -HbA1c-, continuous glucose
monitoring -CGM- metrics), hypoglyce-
mia, weight or body mass index (BMI),
tolerability/adverse events, pregnancy out-
comes, and explicit post-genetic switches
(e.g., from insulin to sulfonylurea -SU-).
Eligibility Criteria
For this review, we included only peer-
reviewed original studies (trials, cohorts, case
series, case reports). Papers were eligible if the
diagnosis of MODY was genetically confirmed,
the treatment was defined, and outcomes were
clearly reported. Additional papers were identi-
fied by a manual search of the references from
the retrieved articles. Conference abstracts
without full text, reviews/editorials/guide-
lines papers, papers without clearly reported
outcomes or insufficient/fragmented data pre-
cluding extraction, and non-English language
manuscripts were excluded. This review uses
only previously published data and does not
include research involving human participants
or animals conducted by the authors.
Two reviewers (LZ, MD) independently
screened titles/abstracts and full texts; disa-
greements were resolved by consensus. Using
a piloted template, we collected: first author,
year, country, study design, genetic test,
MODY gene/variant, sample size, age/sex
where available, treatment(s) and post-genetic
therapy changes, and outcomes (HbA1c and
other clinical endpoints). When multiple time
points were reported, pre/post values related
to therapy changes were extracted.
Outcomes and Synthesis
The primary objective of this paper is to pro-
vide an update about gene-specic treatment
management by summarizing therapeutic
responses and outcomes. The methodological
quality of the included studies was assessed
using the Newcastle–Ottawa Scale (NOS).
Given the inclusion of case reports and case
series, quality assessment was interpreted
descriptively across study designs. The quality
assessment results are displayed in the supple-
mentary material, Tables1–14.
Protocol and Deviations
The methodological framework was consist-
ent with that used in the previously published
review. To further ensure transparency, the
protocol for this updated review has now been
registered in the PROSPERO database (regis-
tration number CRD420261328522). Meth-
ods followed those of the prior review [13],
with an extended time window to October 11,
2025. Final PRISMA counts (records identied/
removed/excluded/included) and any addi-
tional studies retrieved in the last search will be
reported in the Results and the PRISMA ow-
chart. The PRISMA 2020 checklist is provided
as supplementary material (Table15).
Study Selection
The initial search yielded 3856 records. After
removal of duplicates, 3783 titles/abstracts
were screened, and 3426 records were excluded
for not meeting the inclusion criteria. Of 357
full-text articles assessed, 272 were excluded
(topic not fully relevant, neonatal diabetes,
therapy not specied or not evaluable). Six
additional studies were identied through ref-
erence list screening. In total, 91 studies met
all inclusion criteria and were included in the
qualitative synthesis (Fig.1).
To avoid excessive length of the manuscript,
and given the lack of meaningful novelty in
the pathophysiology over the last year, we do
not describe in this paper the genetic defects
Diabetes Ther
Fig. 1 PRISMA ow-chart
Diabetes Ther
and the pathophysiological effects, reported in
our previous paper [13], but just a brief clinical
presentation of each MODY subtype.
This article is based on previously conducted
studies and does not contain new studies with
human participants or animals performed by
any of the authors.
HNF4A‑MODY (Supplementary Material,
Table1)
Individuals with HNF4A-MODY usually have
normal or near-normal glucose tolerance in the
rst decade of life. Glucose intolerance typically
emerges in adolescence/early adulthood along-
side hormonal changes that increase insulin
resistance. In early disease, when fasting glucose
and HbA1c remain in the non-diabetic range,
moderating dietary carbohydrate can reduce
post-prandial excursions. Over time, HbA1c
tends to rise, while ketosis remains uncommon
owing to preserved endogenous insulin secre-
tion. When lifestyle alone is insufcient, SUs in
this genotype, often outperform insulin as rst-
line therapy [2]. SUs bind Sulfonylurea Receptor
1 (SUR1) subunit, closing the potassium ATP-
sensitive (KATP) channel, depolarizing the β-cell
membrane and promoting insulin [14, 15]. Cur-
rent guidance emphasizes the high SU sensitiv-
ity of HNF4A-MODY and supports SUs as pre-
ferred initial pharmacotherapy when feasible [2].
Switches to SUs. Beyond earlier reports
[16–19], additional case-based papers describe
late reclassication to HNF4A-MODY with tran-
sition from insulin to low-dose SU (gliclazide/
glipizide), improving HbA1c and CGM metrics
[20–22]. Across case series and observational
studies, switching from insulin to SUs consist-
ently improves glycemic control (HbA1c 6–7%)
in patients initially misdiagnosed with T1D or
T2D [20–25].
Efficacy and durability of SUs. A recent
HNF4A-MODY series documented clinically
meaningful HbA1c reductions on SU mono-
therapy, with durable control during follow-up;
adjunct agents such as glucagon-like peptide-1
receptor agonists (GLP-1 RAs) and sodium-
glucose co-transporter inhibitors (SGLT2i)
were used when BMI was higher, diabetes was
longer-standing, or insulin secretory capacity
was reduced [26]. In particular, in this large series
(31 patients), 51.6% of the patients showed a
signicant reduction in HbA1c (p = 0.045) dur-
ing SU treatment with stable glycemic control
after 6years of follow-up (IQR: 3.5–11). The
remaining 48.4% of patients required additional
therapies, such as insulin, presenting a higher
BMI (p = 0.037) and a longer duration of diabe-
tes (p = 0.03) [26]. The authors conclude that
patients on just SU (mean dose 120mg/day)
were younger (p = 0.011) and presented a shorter
history of dysglycemia (p = 0.019).
Alternatives or add-on to SUs. Two descrip-
tive case series of four patients, two treated with
GLP-1 RAs and two with dual gastric inhibitory
peptide (GIP)/GLP-1 RAs [27], and two patients,
treated with GLP-1 RAs [28], showed that these
agents are effective to enable down-titration or
even withdrawal of insulin or other oral hypo-
glycemic agents (OHAs). HbA1c decreased by
0.6–1.8% reaching the therapeutic target, BMI
decreased by 1.4–5kg/m
2
and 7.4–10.4kg/m
2
in patients on GLP-1 RAs and dual GIP/GLP-1
RAs, respectively, with overall good tolerability
[26]. Larger improvement (HbA1c −2.5% and
−3.7%) was reported by Broome etal. [28]. Sim-
ilar data were reported by Crowley etal. [26]
in three patients treated with GLP1-RAs who
showed an HbA1c decrease from 76mmol/mol
to 58mmol/mol and a meaningful weight reduc-
tion. Even SGLT2i were shown to be effective in
two patients from the same cohort, despite an
increased risk of genital-urinary infection.
GCK‑MODY (Supplementary Material,
Table2)
GCK-MODY is the most frequent subtype of
MODY worldwide. It is characterized by mild,
stable hyperglycemia since birth. In our previous
review, the studies supported no pharmacologic
therapy for individuals with GCK-MODY [13],
and more recent reports are in line with previous
conclusions. In the majority of patients, diet and
physical exercise are sufcient to manage mild
hyperglycemia with HbA1c only slightly above
the normal range [19, 29–31].
Diabetes Ther
Excluding pregnancy studies, we identied
208 individuals with GCK-MODY: 157 were
managed with diet/exercise, 17 received no ther-
apy, 30 were on OHAs (most commonly met-
formin, n = 12), and 11 were on insulin, which
was discontinued after genetic conrmation in
most of them [18, 29–40].
Studies consistently show that GCK-MODY
is characterized by mild, stable hyperglycemia
at diagnosis (fasting glucose ~ 125 mg/dl, mean
HbA1c around 6–7%). Most patients maintain
adequate glycemic control with diet and lifestyle
measures alone, while the addition of insulin
or oral glucose-lowering agents (metformin, SU,
GLP-1 RA, SGLT2i) provide little or no additional
benet and are often discontinued after genetic
confirmation. These findings are consistent
across adult and pediatric case series, cohorts,
and registries, supporting early molecular diag-
nosis and minimal-intervention management [8,
21–24, 29, 30, 40–44]. This new evidence rein-
forces the “no routine drugs” principle outside
pregnancy and conrms that in these subjects
the molecular diagnosis prevents unnecessary
medication.
Pregnancy
Pregnancy is a special context of increased
insulin resistance and requires gene-aware
management. In a registry analysis of 54
women and 128 pregnancies, 39 insulin-treated
gestations were associated with adverse events
(hypoglycemia in 22 pregnancies, includ-
ing nine severe), whereas no adverse events
were reported in untreated gestations. Among
mutation-positive fetuses, maternal insulin
treatment was associated with lower birth-
weight than non-insulin management; among
mutation-negative fetuses, birthweight did
not differ between treated and untreated preg-
nancies [46]. In a similar study of 34 women,
59% of pregnancies were managed with diet
and 41% with insulin: in GCK-unaffected off-
spring, cesarean section rate, birthweight and
large for gestational age (LGA) prevalence were
higher than in GCK-affected offspring. This is
because the fetus, not inheriting the muta-
tion, perceives maternal blood glucose as “too
high”, thus increases insulin secretion and the
risk of macrosomia. Insulin during pregnancy
did not reduce LGA in unaffected offspring and
was associated with earlier gestational age and
more cesarean sections, with no effect on birth-
weight in affected offspring [47]. A prospective
series of 41 GCK-MODY pregnancies compared
insulin (n = 21) vs. diet (n = 20): hypoglycemia
occurred in a substantial fraction of offspring
in the insulin group, whereas no hypoglyce-
mia or perinatal complications were reported
among offspring from non-insulin pregnan-
cies; birthweight was lower in GCK-affected
newborns when mothers received insulin than
when they did not, whereas unaffected neo-
nates had higher birthweight and 33% were
macrosomic [48].
Contemporary single-case reports illustrate
how CGM can support diet-only management
in most GCK-MODY pregnancies, with time-
in-range improving across gestation and no
increase in maternal hypoglycemia or neonatal
complications in a pregnant woman from China
[49]. Familial GCK-MODY reports similarly
emphasize that, outside pregnancy, pharma-
cological therapy is generally unnecessary and
that intrapartum insulin should be reserved for
suspected non-carrier fetuses with overgrowth
[24, 25, 45].
A novel oral hypoglycemic agent, dorzagli-
atin provides proof-of-concept for precision
treatment in GCK-MODY. It is a rst-in-class
dual-acting glucokinase activator approved in
China for the treatment of adults with T2D. It
restores glucose sensitivity in pancreatic β-cells
and enhances hepatic glucose utilization,
thereby targeting a fundamental defect in glu-
cose homeostasis. In a double-blind randomized
placebo-controlled crossover study, eight adults
with GCK-MODY (7F/1M; age 36.1 ± 6.4years;
HbA1c 6.7 ± 0.3%) underwent a 2-h hypergly-
cemic clamp after a single 75mg oral dose.
Dorzagliatin lowered basal glucose versus pla-
cebo (4.6 ± 0.6 vs. 5.4 ± 0.3mmol/l) and signi-
cantly improved second-phase insulin secretion
and β-cell glucose sensitivity, with no serious
adverse events (one mild hypoglycemia treated
with i.v. dextrose). In an 8-year follow-up case
report, dorzagliatin 75mg twice daily reduced
HbA1c from 7.4 to 6.3% at 3months and 6.1%
Diabetes Ther
at 6months, with no hypoglycemic events
reported [50, 51].
HNF1A‑MODY (Supplementary Material,
Table3)
HNF1A-MODY typically presents in adolescence
or early adulthood with progressive hypergly-
cemia in non-obese, autoantibody-negative
individuals. A key feature is a reduced renal
threshold for glucose reabsorption, leading to
glycosuria at relatively low plasma glucose lev-
els. Glycemia often shows normal or mildly ele-
vated fasting glucose initially, with early post-
prandial hyperglycemia, followed by a gradual
deterioration over time. Microvascular com-
plications may occur; therefore, carbohydrate-
moderated nutrition and early SUs therapy are
recommended [2]. In early disease, satisfactory
control has been reported with dietary interven-
tion alone in some carriers [29, 32, 36]. A recent
Turkish pediatric MODY cohort also reported
four HNF1A-MODY cases mainly managed with
diet or metformin rather than sulfonylureas,
highlighting heterogeneous real-world treat-
ment patterns [42]. As disease progresses, SUs
are highly effective and often provide better
glycemic control than insulin, even in pediatric
cases [52]. Dosing should start low (about one-
quarter of typical starting doses) and can often
be maintained at low maintenance doses (e.g.,
gliclazide 20–40mg/day) [53, 54]. Interestingly,
some recent papers explore the safety and ef-
cacy of other OHAs in the treatment of these
patients.
The superiority of SU-based therapy has been
conrmed by multiple studies and case series;
after genetic diagnosis, switch to SU improves
glycemic control [18, 38, 55–60]. Durable, low-
dose SU regimens have been reported in adoles-
cents and young adults with sustained HbA1c
between 5.5 and 6.9% on glibenclamide/glime-
piride, sometimes after initial insulin expo-
sure [48, 61, 62]. A structured switch program
showed benet across pathogenic HNF1A vari-
ants and, in 6/9 with likely-pathogenic/vari-
ant of unknown significance (VUS), despite
prior insulin use; non-responders often had
absent endogenous insulin or T2D criteria [63].
Similar ndings were reported in other ances-
tries [64–66].
The GLIMLINA study, a randomized, double-
blind, crossover trial, investigated the effect
of linagliptin add-on to glimepiride (vs. pla-
cebo add-on) on fasting glucose, glycemic vari-
ability, and HbA1c [68]. The authors randomly
assigned 19 adult patients (HbA1c 7.4 ± 6 0.2%
[57.1 ± 7.3mmol/mol]) to treatment with glime-
piride and linagliptin 5mg for 16weeks, 4-week
washout, and then glimepiride and placebo for
16weeks [or vice versa). They found that glime-
piride + linagliptin did not signicantly improve
the mean amplitude of glycemic excursions
(MAGE) as compared to glimepiride and pla-
cebo, but signicantly reduced the coefcient
of variation on CGM, the HbA1c (without any
difference in hypoglycemic events), the glime-
piride dose, and nally improved the beta-cell
glucose sensitivity.
Repaglinide has been effective in SU-intol-
erant patients, improving HbA1c when other
agents failed [69]. Adding a SGLT2i to gli-
clazide + metformin improved glycemia and
enabled dose reduction of other OHAs in poorly
controlled patients [47, 70].
The efficacy of SGLT2i was evaluated by
Maagensen etal. [71] in a randomized, 25mg
empagliozin versus placebo double-blind study,
through a crossover two three-step hyperglyce-
mic clamps targeted at 1-h periods of 10, 14,
and 18mmol/l glucose in 11 adult patients
with MODY and ten with T2D. The similar
effect on urinary glucose excretion between the
two groups (urinary glucose excretion in the
patients with MODY 24.5g, 95% CI 20.6–28.3),
indicates that SGLT2i as a potential treatment
option. Small mechanistic and clinical studies
also suggest a useful adjunctive role for SGLT2i
in HNF1A-MODY: empagliozin produced com-
parable increases in glucosuria in HNF1A-MODY
and in T2D during a hyperglycemic clamp, with-
out genotype-specic safety signals in this small
sample [72], and single-patient reports have
documented HbA1c and weight benets with
SGLT2i either before or after genetic conrma-
tion [73, 74].
A detailed case report of newly diagnosed
HNF1A-MODY after euglycemic diabetic ketoaci-
dosis on dapagliflozin described a practical
Diabetes Ther
sequence: post-resolution control with basal/
bolus insulin, then de-intensification to gli-
clazide 30mg/day + linagliptin 5mg/day after
genetic conrmation, with HbA1c improving
from 10.2 to 6.3%, reinforcing SU sensitivity
and cautious use of SGLT2i in undifferentiated
young-onset diabetes [73].
Contemporary case series/reports extend the
mutational and phenotypic spectrum, further
supporting a precision-medicine approach [74,
75]. Multi-gene pediatric and adult cohorts and
registry analyses likewise show that, after molec-
ular diagnosis, many individuals with HNF1A-
MODY can be managed with low-dose SUs or
other oral agents with good glycemic control
and progressive de-escalation of insulin, while
newer injectable agents such as GLP-1 RAs or
dual GIP/GLP-1 agonists may further improve
HbA1c and weight in selected individuals [23,
27, 41–44, 76]. Combined use of insulin and
OHAs may be appropriate in some cases to opti-
mize blood glucose control [77].
PDX1‑MODY (Supplementary Material,
Table4)
PDX1-MODY is phenotypically heterogeneous
and presents in adolescence or early adulthood
with mild to moderate hyperglycemia. The
disease course is slowly progressive, reecting
impaired insulin secretion rather than insulin
resistance. Some mutation carriers may also
show subtle exocrine pancreatic dysfunction or
a family history of early-onset diabetes. Con-
temporary population-based genetic data show
that treatment ranges from diet/OHAs to insulin
when needed [44, 78, 79], supporting a preci-
sion-medicine approach anchored in molecular
diagnosis, phenotype, and treatment response.
Management is individualized, starting with
medical nutrition therapy and physical activ-
ity. Low-dose SUs can be effective in selected
cases (e.g., sustained control with gliclazide in
a 28-year-old man) [80]. Dipeptidyl peptidase-4
(DPP-4) inhibitors may help address post-pran-
dial hyperglycemia: an 18-year-old maintained
HbA1c between 6.0 and 6.5% rst on gliclazide
40mg/day, then on sitagliptin 50mg/day. His
father carried the same variant but achieved
HbA1c 6.3–6.5% with diet alone [81]. Metformin
may be reasonable when there is evidence of
insulin resistance. In one patient with HbA1c
13.6%, it reduced HbA1c to < 7%, and later insu-
lin add-on restored control when HbA1c rose
again [82].
In the Diabetes Patienten Verlaufsdokumen-
tation (DPV) registry, 13 patients with PDX1-
MODY (38.5% with positive anti beta-cell anti-
bodies) showed good overall glycemic control,
with mean HbA1c 6.0% in the rst year after
diagnosis and 6.5% at last visit, a median daily
insulin dose of 0.6IU/kg, and a heterogeneous
treatment pattern including insulin therapy in
30.8%, OHA/GLP-1-based therapy in 38.5%,
metformin in 15.4%, glinides in 15.4%, SUs in
15.4%, and lifestyle-only management in 38.5%
[76].
HNF1B‑MODY (Supplementary Material,
Table5)
HNF1B-MODY is characterized by early-onset
diabetes with variable hyperglycemia, often
requiring insulin early due to impaired insu-
lin secretion. It is frequently associated with
renal abnormalities (cysts), hypomagnesemia,
and urogenital or liver involvement. Diabe-
tes progression is heterogeneous and reects
a multisystem disorder rather than isolated
β-cell dysfunction. The treatment of patients
with HNF1B-MODY has often been challeng-
ing, as responses to the proposed therapeutic
approaches have been highly variable.
Recent series advocate insulin as first-line
in many cases, conrming previous data and
reporting good control when used early and
tailored to individual renal/metabolic status
[83–88]. A Brazilian case report highlighted the
difculty of control (HbA1c 12.1%) despite ther-
apy with very high insulin requirements (1.7IU/
kg/day) [89].
GLP-1 RAs are increasingly reported as insu-
lin-sparing options [90]. A 17-year-old reduced
multiple daily insulin dose from 1.0 to 0.4IU/
kg/day after switching to semaglutide 0.5mg
weekly, with HbA1c falling from 7.0% to 5.9%,
time in range (TIR) rising from 70 to 88% and
time below range (TBR) decreasing from 5 to
Diabetes Ther
1%. The treatment was stopped at 4months for
gastrointestinal adverse effects [91]. In another
report, liraglutide added to basal insulin rapidly
improved TIR (from 39 to 65% in 6days) and
maintained HbA1c < 7% on continued therapy
[92]. A further report in a patient showed HbA1c
and weight benets with GLP-1 RAs or dual GIP/
GLP-1 RAs [27]. These observations indicate
that the GLP-1 RAs may be used as adjuncts or
temporary alternatives in HNF1B-MODY when
weight, variability or hypoglycemia are con-
cerns, recognizing that tolerability and dura-
bility may limit long-term use and that insulin
often remains necessary.
NEUROD1‑MODY (Supplementary Material,
Table6)
NEUROD1-MODY has a variable age of onset,
from childhood to adulthood. It is caused by
impaired β-cell differentiation and insulin
secretion with heterogeneous severity, ranging
from mild hyperglycemia to insulin-requiring
diabetes. Incomplete penetrance is described
and clinical expression is heterogeneous, even
within families, and as a result not all carriers
develop diabetes [93, 94]. When hyperglycemia
occurs, medical nutrition therapy is founda-
tional. OHAs can be effective in a substantial
subset, approximately half in published series/
reviews, often as monotherapy or dual therapy
(e.g., SUs, metformin, DPP-4 inhibitors) [94].
Insulin should be used when glycemic targets
are not achieved with OHAs or when β-cell dys-
function is advanced. In a recent nationwide
Japanese cohort, Yoshiji etal. identied only
one adolescent with NEUROD1-MODY (diag-
nosed at 15years, HbA1c 6.9%) among 67 car-
riers of pathogenic or likely pathogenic MODY
variants, and individual treatment details were
not reported, underscoring how rare and poorly
characterized this subtype remains [44]. An illus-
trative report described a 48-year-old misdiag-
nosed as T2D who failed multiple non-insulin
agents and ultimately required basal insulin to
achieve HbA1c around 7.5%, underscoring vari-
able drug responsiveness in NEUROD1-MODY
[95].
KLF11‑MODY (Supplementary Material,
Table7)
The clinical evidence base is sparse and het-
erogeneous, and reported pedigrees show
phenotypes overlapping other diabetes forms
[96, 97]. Presentations span from early-onset
insulin-treated diabetes to milder dysglycemia
manageable with OHAs. Insulin has often been
started early in published cases [96]. Conversely,
metformin-based management can sufce in
selected carriers: Zhang etal. reported a proband
whose HbA1c improved from 9.5% to 6% with
metformin plus diet. The patient’s mother and
grandmother (both KLF11 carriers) were also
maintained on metformin [97]. Given the rarity
and variability, therapy should be individualized
after genetic conrmation, with periodic reas-
sessment of progression and treatment response.
Additional cohort and registry data provide
a more granular picture of KLF11-related diabe-
tes. In a Turkish pediatric MODY cohort (n = 44)
including a broad spectrum of genes, two KLF11
mutation carriers (4.5%) were identied; one
was treated with insulin from diagnosis and
the other started on metformin before requir-
ing insulin, against a background of markedly
elevated HbA1c in non-GCK subtypes (mean
9.48 ± 3.75%) and frequent use of combined
OHAs and insulin [42]. In the DPV registry,
KLF11-MODY remained rare but showed rela-
tively high treatment intensity, with all carri-
ers on insulin therapy, mean HbA1c decreasing
from 7.6% in the rst year to 7.2% at last visit,
and one-third also receiving metformin and
16.7% an SGLT2i, while no use of SUs, GLP-1
RAs or other OHAs was reported [76].
CEL‑MODY (Supplementary Material,
Table8)
Diabetes usually develops in the third to fourth
decade, frequently in association with pancre-
atic cysts [98]. In early or mild hyperglycemia,
diet and exercise can be sufcient for glycemic
control in selected individuals [29]. When phar-
macotherapy is required, available reports sug-
gest that insulin is often the most appropriate
Diabetes Ther
option, such as in the case report by Kondoh
etal. who described excellent control (HbA1c
5.5%) with 0.5IU/kg/day of insulin [99]. Given
the limited evidence base, therapy should be
individualized and guided by longitudinal
assessment of glycemia and exocrine pancreatic
involvement [98–100].
In a Turkish pediatric cohort, four CEL muta-
tion carriers were identied, with one managed
on diet alone, one on insulin only, and two
requiring combined metformin plus insulin [42].
PAX4‑MODY (Supplementary Material,
Table9)
Reported presentations range from childhood
to adult onset, with variable severity, from diet-
controlled or OHA-treated hyperglycemia to
insulin-requiring diabetes.
In consideration of the rarity of the disease,
the treatment experience is limited. In a large
Brazilian cohort (14 carriers), outcomes were
heterogeneous: some remained untreated with
HbA1c 5.1–5.5%, one maintained control on
diet alone, others used unspecied OHAs with
divergent results (HbA1c 11.3% vs. 6.0%), and
three required multiple daily injections (MDI)
(HbA1c 9.6% and 7.4%; one not available) [89].
A 19-month-old boy achieved adequate control
on MDI, illustrating that early insulin may be
required in pediatric presentations [101].
INS‑MODY (Supplementary Material,
Table10)
INS-MODY is caused by a defective insulin pro-
duction or processing. It presents with variable
age of onset, from childhood to early adulthood,
and progressive hyperglycemia due to β-cell dys-
function. Short-term benet from medical nutri-
tion therapy and, in selected cases, OHAs may
occur, but many individuals ultimately require
insulin therapy as β-cell function progressively
declines [102, 103]. In the paper by Schlegel
etal., intensifying from metformin + basal glar-
gine to include rapid-acting aspart improved
HbA1c from 10.6 to 6.9% in 3months [104].
In selected insulin-treated cases, adjunct ther-
apy can assist. Empagliozin (25mg/day) as
add on treatment to MDI showed to be effec-
tive decreasing HbA1c from 18.6 to 8.6% in
a 17-year-old boy [105]. A recent case report
described an adolescent who initially required
high-dose insulin + metformin. After 2.5years
and weight loss, metabolic control normalized
and pharmacotherapy (insulin and metformin)
was discontinued, highlighting phenotypic vari-
ability and the possibility of de-intensication
in selected scenarios [106]. Pediatric and adult
multi-gene cohorts indicate that many individu-
als are managed with insulin from diagnosis, but
that non-insulin agents such as metformin, SUs,
or GLP-1 RAs are sometimes used as adjuncts or
in milder phenotypes [23, 41, 43, 44, 76].
Cohort and registry data provide additional
granularity. In a Chinese pediatric program,
six patients with INS-MODY were mostly man-
aged with long-term insulin, with individual
examples showing HbA1c improving from 10.8
to 6.6%, from 8.3 to 6.5%, from 14.5 to 6.4%,
from 12.0 to 8.5%, and from 15.3 to 6.8%, while
one case worsened from 8.9 to 11.3% [41]. In
the DPV registry, INS-MODY remained rare but
treatment was intensive, with mean HbA1c 6.9%
in the rst year and 7.2% at last visit, 71.4%
receiving insulin therapy, 28.6% having OHA/
GLP-1-based treatment (including 14.3% on SUs
and 14.3% on GLP-1 RAs) and 28.6% recorded
as lifestyle therapy [76]. Similarly, in a Chinese
single-center series, six of seven patients with
INS-MODY remained on insulin and one on
metformin after genetic diagnosis, despite many
other non-GCK MODY cases being able to dis-
continue insulin [43].
BLK‑MODY (Supplementary Material,
Table11)
In these patients, glycemic derangement is vari-
able. While many patients ultimately require
insulin, some can be managed with medical
nutrition therapy and/or OHAs, particularly
early in the disease course or in milder pheno-
types [107]. Given limited evidence and variable
penetrance, treatment should be individualized
after genetic conrmation, with attention to
weight management and life-stage triggers (e.g.,
pregnancy). Pediatric cohorts provide further
Diabetes Ther
insight into BLK-related diabetes. In a Turkish
cohort, 2 BLK mutation carriers were identi-
ed, with one managed on metformin and one
on diet alone [42]. In a Chinese single-center
series, one BLK-MODY child was treated with
combined insulin and metformin after genetic
diagnosis, suggesting that BLK-MODY often
requires at least oral pharmacotherapy and may
sometimes necessitate insulin [43].
ABCC8‑MODY (Supplementary Material,
Table12)
ABCC8-MODY presents with variable age of
onset and severity, from mild hyperglycemia to
overt diabetes [108] and risk of severe complica-
tions [109]. A key feature is marked sensitivity to
SUs, which are often effective. Some mutations
overlap with neonatal diabetes phenotypes,
reecting broad clinical heterogeneity.
In line with guidelines, after genetic diagnosis,
many individuals can be switched from insulin
(or other OHAs) to SUs with signicant improve-
ment in glycemic control [110–117]. In a pedi-
atric series, most carriers of ABCC8/KCNJ11
variants diagnosed in individuals younger than
3years successfully transitioned from insulin to
oral SUs with clinical benet [118]. Case series
also note that responses to SUs can be dura-
ble yet variable across individuals [109], and
in some of them the dose requirements may
decrease over time, occasionally allowing tem-
porary discontinuation [119]. In selected mild
presentations, dietary management alone has
yielded satisfactory control [29, 120]. As a non-
SU option, GLP-1 RAs have shown benet in
ABCC8-MODY, improving glycemia with weight
loss and a low hypoglycemia risk [121].
KCNJ11‑MODY (Supplementary Material,
Table13)
The β-cell mechanism and the phenotype par-
allels ABCC8, as these genes encode for differ-
ent subunits of the same channel. Most reports
indicate that switching from insulin/OHAs
to SU after genetic conrmation, with signi-
cantly improved glycemic outcomes [122–125].
In selected cases, diet-first approaches have
maintained control without contraindica-
tions [29, 126]. Heterogeneous phenotypes are
described, including presentations with systemic
comorbidities (e.g., pituitary stalk interruption
syndrome, liver disease) for which long-term
glycemic follow-up has been limited in the lit-
erature [127]. During pregnancy, temporary
re-introduction of insulin may be required. Re-
challenge with glibenclamide in later trimesters
has been reported without complications in
carefully selected cases [124].
APPL1‑MODY (Supplementary Material,
Table14)
Reported pedigrees show adult-onset diabetes
with autosomal-dominant transmission and
variable severity. Diet, OHAs, and insulin have
all been used, with treatment individualized to
glycemic severity and progression. In two APPL1
families (24 carriers), seven required insulin, four
were managed with diet alone, and two used
OHAs [128]. In the Turkish pediatric cohort, a
single APPL1-MODY case was identied: it was a
15.3-year-old lean boy with incidental hypergly-
cemia, a strong three-generation family history
of diabetes, negative β-cell autoantibodies and a
heterozygous APPL1 NM_012096.3:c.2018C > G
(p.S673C) variant classied as VUS, managed
with combined metformin and insulin [42].
DISCUSSION
This systematic review updates and expands
the therapeutic landscape of MODY, highlight-
ing relevant advances since our previous review
[13]. While the central role of genotype-guided
therapy remains unchanged, recent evidence
provides important insights into treatment dura-
bility, adjunctive pharmacological options, and
emerging precision therapies that may further
rene individualized management. Table1 sum-
marizes some practical guidance for the thera-
peutic management of patients with MODY and
has been developed to assist clinicians in clinical
practice. In addition, given the variable strength
of evidence across MODY subtypes, Table1 was
designed to provide readers with an overview of
Diabetes Ther
Table 1 Practical suggestions for managements of patients with MODY
Gene Practice-oriented notes Strength of evidence
HNF4A Recent evidence reinforces SUs as rst-line treatment,
with GLP-1-based therapies as reasonable alterna-
tives or add-ons when SUs intolerance, hypoglycemia
risk, or weight management considerations arise
Insulin is generally reserved for pregnancy (when
indicated) or advanced β-cell failure
Moderate/consistent
GCK Recent papers reinforce the suggestion to not treat
these individuals with any hypoglycemic agents
Pregnancy management should be guided by fetal
genotype and growth: insulin is generally not indi-
cated when the fetus is GCK-aected (risk of growth
restriction) and may be considered when the fetus
is unaected and shows excess growth. When fetal
genotype is unknown, early CGM can support a con-
servative, growth-aware prandial insulin approach,
targeting the upper end of pregnancy glucose ranges
and focusing on post-prandial control to curb
macrosomia if the fetus is unaected while avoiding
over-tight control if aected
Dorzagliatin, a GCK activator licensed for human
use only in China, is an emerging option in GCK-
MODY, supported by proof-of-concept clamp data
showing improved second-phase insulin secretion
and β-cell glucose sensitivity versus placebo with no
serious adverse events, and by a case report describing
HbA1c reduction aer failure of multiple therapies
with treatment reported as safe under biochemical
monitoring
Moderate/consistent for conservative manage-
ment; preliminary for dorzagliatin
HNF1A Start with low-dose SU and titrate carefully; consider
DPP-4 inhibitors as add-on to reduce variability or
SU dose, repaglinide when SU are not tolerated,
and SGLT2i or GLP-1-RA in selected scenarios
(weight, hypoglycemia, cardio-renal risk), with
insulin reserved for pregnancy when indicated or for
advanced β-cell failure
Moderate/consistent
PDX1 Start with diet-rst and weight-sensitive strategies;
consider SU or DPP-4 inhibitor for post-prandial
control. Use metformin when insulin resistance is
present and insulin if targets are not met or β-cell
function declines
Preliminary/case-based
Diabetes Ther
Table 1 continued
Gene Practice-oriented notes Strength of evidence
HNF1B Anticipate reduced SU sensitivity and a lower thresh-
old for insulin initiation. Manage comorbid kidney
disease proactively; coordinate nephrology, hepatol-
ogy, and lipid care. Consider GLP-1RA (and/or
DPP-4 inhibitors) as adjuncts to reduce insulin dose,
improve TIR and support weight control, monitor-
ing for gastrointestinal adverse eects and reassessing
need for insulin as disease progresses
Limited
NEUROD1 Start with diet/lifestyle, consider SU, metformin, or
DPP-4 inhibitors based on phenotype and tolerabil-
ity; escalate to insulin when needed
Preliminary/case-based
KLF11 Consider metformin (± lifestyle) in mild, stable hyper-
glycemia; use insulin when hyperglycemia is marked
or progressive
Preliminary/case-based
CEL Consider lifestyle-only management in mild disease;
escalate to insulin when targets are unmet or hyper-
glycemia progresses. Monitor exocrine function and
pancreatic imaging features over time; management
remains case-by-case given scarce treatment data
Preliminary/case-based
PAX4 Start with medical nutrition therapy; consider OHA
in mild, stable hyperglycemia; use insulin when
targets are unmet or in more severe/early-onset
phenotypes
Preliminary/case-based
INS Use CGM-guided titration and early basal-bolus opti-
mization; consider adjuncts selectively, with vigilance
for euglycemic ketosis risk in insulin-decient states
Limited; supported by broader neonatal diabetes
evidence
BLK While many patients ultimately require insulin, some
can be managed with medical nutrition therapy and/
or OHAs, particularly early in the disease course or
in milder phenotypes. Given limited evidence and
variable penetrance, treatment should be individual-
ized aer genetic conrmation, with attention to
weight management and life-stage triggers (e.g.,
pregnancy)
Preliminary/case-based
ABCC8 Prioritize SU switch aer molecular conrmation;
monitor dose requirements and long-term durability.
Consider GLP-1RA as adjuncts or alternatives when
SU is poorly tolerated or weight loss is desirable
Limited to moderate in MODY; supported by
broader KATP-channel diabetes evidence
Diabetes Ther
the available evidence and to facilitate interpre-
tation of the therapeutic suggestions.
Among the most extensively studied subtypes,
HNF1A-MODY and HNF4A-MODY continue to
demonstrate marked sensitivity to SUs, con-
rming their role as rst-line pharmacological
therapy [2, 18, 20–25, 38, 52, 55–60, 64–67].
However, recent data provide novel insights
into predictors of long-term SU efcacy. Treat-
ment durability appears strongly influenced
by clinical and metabolic factors, including
younger age at diagnosis, shorter diabetes dura-
tion, and preserved β-cell function [27, 52, 63,
67]. These ndings reinforce the importance of
early molecular diagnosis, as delayed identi-
cation may reduce the likelihood of sustained
response to SU therapy.
One randomized crossover trial, small obser-
vational studies, and case series indicate that
incretin-based agents may represent useful
adjunctive or alternative options in selected
individuals with HNF1A- and HNF4A-MODY.
Case series and observational studies have
reported meaningful reductions in HbA1c
and body weight, along with reduced insulin
requirements or successful insulin discontinu-
ation following initiation of GLP-1 RAs or dual
GIP/GLP-1 RAs [23, 26, 27, 41–44, 76]. The
GLIMLINA randomized crossover trial further
demonstrated that linagliptin added to glime-
piride improved glycemic variability, HbA1c,
and β-cell glucose sensitivity without increasing
hypoglycemia risk, indicating the potential role
of incretin-based combination therapy in opti-
mizing metabolic control [68]. These ndings
suggest that incretin-based agents may represent
promising adjunctive therapies in selected indi-
viduals with progressive β-cell dysfunction or
emerging insulin resistance, although evidence
derives mainly from small studies and case-based
reports.
Similarly, emerging data suggest a possible
adjunctive role for SGLT2i in selected patients;
however, current evidence remains limited and
should be interpreted with caution. Empaglio-
zin induces signicant glucosuria and improves
glycemic control in HNF1A-MODY, with effects
comparable to those observed in T2D [71, 72].
Clinical reports also suggest benets in HbA1c
reduction, weight control, and treatment
Table 1 continued
Gene Practice-oriented notes Strength of evidence
KCNJ11 Expect substantial SU responsiveness aer genetic
diagnosis; titrate carefully to minimize hypoglyce-
mia. Use diet/lifestyle in mild disease; reserve insulin
for pregnancy, intercurrent illness, or inadequate SU
response. Remain vigilant for neurological involve-
ment in KCNJ11 disease and tailor counseling
accordingly
Limited to moderate in MODY; supported by
broader KATP-channel diabetes evidence
APPL1 Tailor therapy (diet/OHAs/insulin) to phenotype and
progression; reassess periodically. Consider con-
comitant insulin-resistance pathways when selecting
OHAs, given APPL1’s role in adiponectin-insulin
signaling
Preliminary/case-based
HNF4A hepatocyte nuclear factor 4α, GCK glucokinase, HNF1A hepatocyte nuclear factor 1α, PDX1 pancreatic and
duodenal homeobox 1, HNF1B hepatocyte nuclear factor 1β, NEUROD1 neurogenic dierentiation 1, KLF11 Krup-
pel-like factor 11, CEL carboxyl ester lipase, PAX4 paired box4, INS insulin, BLK B lymphoid tyrosine kinase, ABCC8
ATP-binding cassette subfamily C member 8, KCNJ11 potassium inwardly-rectifying channel subfamily J member 11, SU
sulfonylurea(s), GLP-1 RA glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist(s), CGM continuous glucose monitoring, HbA1c hemo-
globin A1c, DPP-4 dipeptidyl peptidase-4, SGLT2i sodium–glucose co-transporter-2 inhibitor(s), TIR time in range, OHA
oral hypoglycemic agent, KATP potassium ATP-sensitive channel
Diabetes Ther
simplication when used alone or in combina-
tion with other agents [47, 70, 73, 74]. How-
ever, safety considerations, particularly the risk
of diabetic ketoacidosis, require careful patient
selection and monitoring [72]. These ndings
support a more flexible, phenotype-guided
approach.
Recent evidence also highlights therapeutic
advances in HNF1B-MODY, a subtype tradition-
ally characterized by early insulin dependence.
While insulin remains the cornerstone of treat-
ment [83–88], incretin-based therapies have
shown promising insulin-sparing effects in
selected cases. Case reports describe reductions
in insulin requirements and improvements in
glycemic control, TIR, and body weight follow-
ing initiation of GLP-1 RAs [29, 89, 91], in line
with our personal observation (unpublished
data). These observations suggest a possible
adjunctive role for incretin-based therapies in
improving metabolic outcomes and reducing
treatment burden, although evidence remains
limited to case-based experience.
For GCK-MODY, recent evidence largely con-
firms previous recommendations supporting
conservative management without pharmaco-
logical therapy in most individuals [19, 29–45].
However, an important novel development
is the emergence of glucokinase activators as
potential precision therapies. Dorzagliatin, a
dual-acting GCK activator currently approved in
China for T2D, represents a promising targeted
therapy, although current evidence remains
limited to proof-of-concept data and case-
level experience [50, 51]. While clinical expe-
rience remains limited, this agent represents a
promising example of targeted therapy directly
addressing the underlying molecular defect. Fur-
ther studies are needed to clarify its long-term
efcacy, safety, and applicability across diverse
populations.
In pregnancy, recent observational studies fur-
ther rene genotype-driven management strat-
egies. Evidence continues to support individu-
alized treatment based on fetal genotype and
growth patterns, with insulin therapy reserved
for unaffected fetuses showing evidence of
overgrowth [46–48]. In addition, advances in
diabetes technologies, particularly continuous
glucose monitoring, may provide valuable tools
to optimize glycemic control while minimizing
overtreatment and associated risks [49]. These
developments contribute to a more personalized
and physiologically appropriate management
approach during pregnancy.
Recent data suggest possible therapeutic appli-
cations for MODY subtypes associated with
ABCC8 and KCNJ11 gene mutations. Consist-
ent with previous knowledge, individuals with
these mutations often exhibit marked SU sensi-
tivity and can successfully transition from insu-
lin to oral therapy following genetic diagnosis
[110–117, 121–123]. Emerging evidence also
suggests a potential adjunctive role for incretin-
based therapies in selected cases, particularly
when additional metabolic benefits such as
weight reduction are desired [30, 118–120, 124].
Recent reports conrm that insulin therapy
remains the mainstay of treatment for INS-
MODY due to progressive β-cell dysfunction
[102, 103], but emerging evidence suggests that
adjunctive therapies, including metformin,
SGLT2i, and incretin-based agents, may provide
temporary metabolic benets and facilitate treat-
ment de-intensication in selected individuals
[76, 104–106]. These observations highlight the
phenotypic heterogeneity of INS-MODY and
suggest that individualized therapeutic strate-
gies may delay, but not prevent, eventual insulin
dependence.
We highlight that for ABCC8-, KCNJ11- and
INS-MODY, the available MODY-specic evi-
dence is relatively limited, although it is bio-
logically and clinically supported by the more
robust literature on neonatal diabetes.
Evidence remains limited for rarer MODY
subtypes associated with PDX1, NEUROD1,
CEL, KLF11, PAX4, and APPL1 gene mutations.
However, recent cohort and registry data rein-
force the importance of individualized man-
agement based on clinical phenotype, typi-
cally involving lifestyle interventions, OHAs,
and eventual insulin therapy as β-cell dysfunc-
tion progresses [42, 44, 76, 129, 130]. An addi-
tional contributor to clinical heterogeneity is
represented by digenic inheritance, which may
result in variable phenotypic expression and
therapeutic response, further emphasizing the
need for individualized treatment approaches
guided by clinical and genetic characteristics
Diabetes Ther
[131]. Collectively, the most relevant thera-
peutic advances include the expanding role
of incretin-based therapies as adjunctive treat-
ments, the emergence of SGLT2i as potential
add-on options, and the development of tar-
geted therapies such as GCK activators. These
innovations reect a shift toward increasingly
individualized and mechanism-based treat-
ment strategies.
Despite these advances, important limitations
in the available evidence persist. Most therapeu-
tic data derive from observational studies, case
series, and small cohorts, with few randomized
clinical trials. Furthermore, heterogeneity in
study design, outcome measures, and follow-up
duration limits the ability to establish denitive
treatment algorithms for several subtypes. Taken
together, all these factors result in limited robust
evidence in the literature for several MODY sub-
types. Nevertheless, the overall consistency of
genotype-specic treatment responses across
studies supports the validity of precision medi-
cine approaches in MODY management. From a
methodological point of view, it could be argued
that a potential limitation of this review is that
the literature search was conducted only in Pub-
Med; however, this choice ensured methodologi-
cal continuity with our previously published
review, and manual screening of reference lists
was performed to minimize the risk of missing
relevant studies. Accordingly, the therapeu-
tic suggestions reported in this review should
be interpreted with caution, as the underlying
strength of evidence varies substantially across
MODY subtypes, ranging from relatively consist-
ent data in some settings to limited or explora-
tory evidence in others.
We believe that beyond diagnostic screen-
ing, systematic investigation of the molecular
and cellular mechanisms underlying β-cell dys-
function in MODY is essential to identify novel
therapeutic targets and further advance preci-
sion medicine in monogenic diabetes. Future
research should prioritize prospective studies,
randomized clinical trials, and international
collaborative registries to strengthen the evi-
dence base and refine treatment algorithms.
Deeper investigation into the molecular mecha-
nisms underlying β-cell dysfunction may iden-
tify novel, pathway-specic therapeutic targets
and enable the development of more effective,
mechanism-based treatments.
Continued advances in molecular diagnostics
and therapeutic development are expected to
further improve individualized care and clini-
cal outcomes in this heterogeneous group of
patients, reinforcing the role of monogenic dia-
betes as a model for precision endocrinology.
CONCLUSIONS
Recent literature conrms knowledge on the
therapeutic management of MODY and rein-
forces the current genotype–phenotype treat-
ment recommendations. In particular, the cen-
tral role of genotype-guided therapy, such as
SUs in HNF1A-, HNF4A-, ABCC8-, and KCNJ11-
MODY, and the conservative approach in GCK-
MODY, is consistently supported by observa-
tional and clinical evidence. At the same time,
emerging studies suggest potentially useful ther-
apeutic options in selected patients. Evidence for
newer therapeutic approaches (incretin-based
therapies, SGLT2i, and GCK activators), which
may offer additional benets in selected patients
and further expand the precision medicine
approach, remains limited and heterogeneous.
However, the strength of evidence differs sub-
stantially across MODY subtypes, being more
consistent for sulfonylurea-based treatment in
HNF1A-, HNF4A-, ABCC8-, and KCNJ11-related
diabetes, and more limited or exploratory for
newer adjunctive therapies and rarer subtypes.
The low prevalence and heterogeneity of MODY
are major barriers to conducting adequately
powered trials. International collaborative
efforts and multicenter registries will therefore
be essential to increase sample sizes, strengthen
the quality of evidence, and rene treatment
recommendations. Hopefully, such initiatives
will ultimately improve individualized care and
support clinicians in optimizing therapeutic
strategies for these rare but clinically important
conditions.

assistance was required. The authors agree that
the edits reect their original work following the
use of AI. We conrm that all authors take full
responsibility for the content of the manuscript
and approve the nal version.
